---
title: "Text Preprocessing"
---

## Why Preprocessing Matters

Raw text contains noise that can mislead analysis: inconsistent capitalisation, punctuation, HTML artefacts, and high-frequency function words that carry little topical signal. Preprocessing converts raw strings into a structured, analysis-ready form.

> Preprocessing choices are consequential. Over-cleaning removes meaningful variation; under-cleaning adds noise. Document your decisions.

## A Standard Pipeline

```
raw text
  → normalise (lowercase, unicode)
  → tokenise
  → remove noise (URLs, HTML, special chars)
  → remove stopwords (optional, task-dependent)
  → stem or lemmatise (optional)
  → construct tokens / DFM object
```

## Normalisation

```{webr}
library(stringr)

raw <- "Breaking: @NATO announces 2024 summit — details at https://t.co/xyz #NATO"

raw |>
  str_to_lower() |>
  str_remove_all("https?://\\S+") |>          # URLs
  str_remove_all("@\\w+") |>                  # mentions
  str_remove_all("#(\\w+)") |>                # hashtags (keep word?)
  str_squish()
```

## Tokenisation

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)

text <- tibble(text = "Breaking: NATO announces 2024 summit in Washington.")

# Word tokens
text |> unnest_tokens(word, text)
```

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)

text <- tibble(text = "Breaking: NATO announces 2024 summit in Washington.")

# N-grams (bigrams)
text |> unnest_ngrams(bigram, text, n = 2)
```

## Stopwords

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)

data("stop_words")

# tidytext ships three lexicons
stop_words |> count(lexicon)
```

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)

texts <- tibble(
  doc = 1:2,
  text = c(
    "The prime minister held a press conference about the economy.",
    "Journalists attended a briefing on the government's new policy."
  )
)

texts |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)
```

## Stemming vs Lemmatisation

**Stemming** (e.g. Porter) crudely chops suffixes: *running → run*, *studies → studi*.
**Lemmatisation** maps to dictionary forms: *running → run*, *studies → study*.

```{webr}
library(quanteda)
library(quanteda.textstats)

toks <- tokens("Journalists are covering several stories about covering government policies.")
# quanteda uses the Snowball stemmer
tokens_wordstem(toks, language = "en")
```

## Building a Document-Feature Matrix

```{webr}
library(quanteda)

docs <- c(
  doc1 = "Media coverage of climate policy intensified after the summit.",
  doc2 = "Politicians criticised media framing of the climate debate.",
  doc3 = "Climate activists welcomed the new policy announcement."
)

dfmat <- corpus(docs) |>
  tokens(remove_punct = TRUE) |>
  tokens_tolower() |>
  tokens_remove(stopwords("en")) |>
  dfm() |>
  dfm_trim(min_termfreq = 1)

dfmat
```

## TF-IDF Weighting

Term frequency–inverse document frequency down-weights terms that appear in many documents and up-weights distinctive terms:

```{webr}
library(tidytext)
library(dplyr)

texts <- tibble(
  doc = c("A", "B", "C"),
  text = c(
    "Media coverage of climate policy intensified after the summit.",
    "Politicians criticised media framing of the climate debate.",
    "Climate activists welcomed the new policy announcement."
  )
)

texts |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  count(doc, word) |>
  bind_tf_idf(word, doc, n) |>
  arrange(desc(tf_idf))
```
